questions.text: I feel that the moral claim of suffering is universal — it applies to me whether the person is a loved one or a stranger.
I find it natural to follow well-given instructions and coordinate with peers.
I hold the view that a detached, information-driven approach to human pain is the method that produces the most defensible and well-reasoned decisions.
I keep promises and follow through even when it becomes inconvenient.
My passions tend to consume me rather than simmer in the background.
I find that I learn about social norms through trial and error rather than intuition.
I am at my best when I am in the driver's seat.
I find lasting satisfaction in contributions that no one knows I made.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is grounded in its moral reality, not in the emotional reaction it produces in me.
I notice when someone's smile fades the instant they think no one is watching.
Even routine goodbyes can stir up a surprising depth of feeling in me.
I routinely decline requests that would compromise my personal goals.
I often set deadlines and hold people to them.
I experience nervousness as a subtle signal rather than a full-body alarm.
I rarely set alarms or reminders; I trust my natural sense of timing.
I save and invest with a long-term financial plan in mind.
I feel that my personal growth matters only insofar as it lets me contribute more to others.
I believe that social environments are rich with information most people overlook.
